Effects of sweet potato feathery mottle virus and sweet potato chlorotic stunt virus on the yield of sweet potato in Uganda
https://hdl.handle.net/10568/72519 Sweet potato feathery mottle virus (SPFMV) and Sweet potato chlorotic stunt virus (SPCSV) are the most common viruses infecting sweetpotato in Uganda. Field plots planted with graft inoculated plants of virus‐free cultivars Beauregard, Dimbuka, Ejumula, Kabode and NASPOT 1 were used to assess the effect of SPFMV and SPCSV on yield and quality of sweetpotatoes in two agro‐ecologies. SPFMV spreads rapidly to control plots at Makerere University Agricultural Research Institute Kabanyolo (MUARIK), and these plots had similar yields to those singly infected with SPFMV but at the National Semi Arid Resource Research Institute (NaSARRI) where SPFMV spreads slowly, plots infected with SPFMV yielded 40% less than the control. Recovery from SPFMV appeared to be more frequent at NaSARRI than at MUARIK. Infection by SPCSV alone resulted in yield losses of 14–52%, while mixed infections of SPFMV+SPCSV resulted in yield losses in both locations of 60–95% depending on the cultivar. SPCSV and mixed infections of SPFMV+SPCSV also reduced the number of roots formed as well as the diameter of the roots, resulting in a greater length to diameter ratio compared to the healthy control. This study, therefore, confirms that both SPFMV and SPCSV, both singly and when mixed, can reduce yield, the extent depending on the cultivar. To mitigate the effect of these viruses, farmers should use clean planting materials of resistant varieties.
Citation: Adikini, S.; Mukasa, S.B.; Mwanga, R.O.M.; Gibson, R.W. 2016. Effects of sweet potato feathery mottle virus and sweet potato chlorotic stunt virus on the yield of sweet potato in Uganda. Article référencé dans le Web of Science WOS:000505240700001. B., Some K., Pita J. To determine the effects of sweet potato feathery mottle virus (SPFMV), Sweet potato chlorotic stunt virus (SPCSV) and their co-infection on sweet potato yield, twelve sweet potato varieties were assessed in a hotspot area in Western Burkina Faso. Varieties Djakani and Ligri were virus-free and had the highest average yields out of twelve sweet potato varieties assessed. The results suggest that severe symptoms were associated with sweet potato virus disease (SPVD) and yield reduction. However, the interaction of SPCSV with other viruses, which may result in synergistic negative effects on sweet potato yield and quality, needs further research."

Sweet potato feathery mottle virus (SPFMV) and Sweet potato chlorotic stunt virus (SPCSV) are the most common viruses infecting sweetpotato in Uganda. Field plots planted with graft inoculated plants of virus‐free cultivars Beauregard, Dimbuka, Ejumula, Kabode and NASPOT 1 were used to assess the effect of SPFMV and SPCSV on yield and quality of sweetpotatoes in two agro‐ecologies. Infection by SPCSV alone resulted in yield losses of 14–52%, while mixed infections of SPFMV+SPCSV resulted in yield losses in both locations of 60–95% depending on the cultivar. file_saver({"file_path": "work_space/gaia_2a649bb1_20251222_003953_920525/analysis_step1_virus_testing_chemicals.md", "content": "Title: Virus testing method chemicals and usage frequency (Adikini et al., 2016)

Source: Effects of Sweet Potato Feathery Mottle Virus and Sweet Potato Chlorotic Stunt Virus on the Yield of SweetPotato in Uganda (Journal of Phytopathology, 2016). Direct PDF: https://cgspace.cgiar.org/bitstream/10568/72519/2/79163.pdf

Method sections inspected:
- Virus testing
- Evaluation of sweetpotato virus disease recovery and virus infection (serology details)

Verbatim extracts (for chemicals and reagents named):
1) Virus testing (page 2):
   - "The leaf samples were tested either using NCM ELISA or DAS and TAS ELISA."
   - "Nitrocellulose membrane ELISA was carried out following the protocol obtained from CIP Lima, Peru..."
   - "Additional tests using DAS and TAS ELISA were specifically carried out... using protocol by Clark and Adams (1977)."
   - "The DAS-ELISA kit (containing coating antibody immunoglobulin G (IgG) and detecting antibody IgG-AP) ... TAS ELISA kit ... were from Leibniz-Institut DSMZ..."
   - "The p-nitrophenyl phosphate substrate and microplates used were from Sigma Chemical Co. The absorbance was measured at 405 nm..."

2) Evaluation of virus infection (page 4):
   - "Samples were collected ... and tested for 10 viruses ... The plants were serologically evaluated at the end of each trial before harvesting."
   - "Positive control samples for each virus were provided in the NCM ELISA Kit by CIP Lima, Peru."

Identified chemicals/materials explicitly named for the virus testing method:
- Nitrocellulose (as "Nitrocellulose membrane" in NCM ELISA)
- p-nitrophenyl phosphate (AP substrate for ELISA)

Other items mentioned (not counted as chemicals for EC lookup):
- Antibodies (IgG, IgG-AP, rabbit anti-mouse IgG-AP; proteins/biologicals)
- Microplates (polystyrene labware)
- ELISA extraction buffer (composition not described)

Frequency assessment within virus testing workflow references:
- Nitrocellulose membrane / NCM ELISA: referenced in both "Virus testing" and again in the "Evaluation of ... virus infection" section (NCM ELISA kit and method). Counted references: 2 sections.
- p-nitrophenyl phosphate substrate: explicitly named once in the Virus testing subsection. Counted references: 1.

Conclusion for step 1:
- The two most commonly used chemicals explicitly named for the virus testing method in the paper are: Nitrocellulose and p-nitrophenyl phosphate.
- These will be taken forward for chemical identity confirmation and EC number lookup in subsequent steps.
"})

Title: Virus testing method chemicals and usage frequency (Adikini et al., 2016)

Source: Effects of Sweet Potato Feathery Mottle Virus and Sweet Potato Chlorotic Stunt Virus on the Yield of SweetPotato in Uganda (Journal of Phytopathology, 2016). Direct PDF: https://cgspace.cgiar.org/bitstream/10568/72519/2/79163.pdf

Method sections inspected:
- Virus testing
- Evaluation of sweetpotato virus disease recovery and virus infection (serology details)

Verbatim extracts (for chemicals and reagents named):
1) Virus testing (page 2):
   - "The leaf samples were tested either using NCM ELISA or DAS and TAS ELISA."
   - "Nitrocellulose membrane ELISA was carried out following the protocol obtained from CIP Lima, Peru..."
   - "Additional tests using DAS and TAS ELISA were specifically carried out... using protocol by Clark and Adams (1977)."
   - "The DAS-ELISA kit (containing coating antibody immunoglobulin G (IgG) and detecting antibody IgG-AP) ... TAS ELISA kit ... were from Leibniz-Institut DSMZ..."
   - "The p-nitrophenyl phosphate substrate and microplates used were from Sigma Chemical Co. The absorbance was measured at 405 nm..."

2) Evaluation of virus infection (page 4):
   - "Samples were collected ... and tested for 10 viruses ... The plants were serologically evaluated at the end of each trial before harvesting."
   - "Positive control samples for each virus were provided in the NCM ELISA Kit by CIP Lima, Peru."

Identified chemicals/materials explicitly named for the virus testing method:
- Nitrocellulose (as "Nitrocellulose membrane" in NCM ELISA)
- p-nitrophenyl phosphate (AP substrate for ELISA)

Other items mentioned (not counted as chemicals for EC lookup):
- Antibodies (IgG, IgG-AP, rabbit anti-mouse IgG-AP; proteins/biologicals)
- Microplates (polystyrene labware)
- ELISA extraction buffer (composition not described)

Frequency assessment within virus testing workflow references:
- Nitrocellulose membrane / NCM ELISA: referenced in both "Virus testing" and again in the "Evaluation of ... virus infection" section (NCM ELISA kit and method). Counted references: 2 sections.
- p-nitrophenyl phosphate substrate: explicitly named once in the Virus testing subsection. Counted references: 1.

Conclusion for step 1:
- The two most commonly used chemicals explicitly named for the virus testing method in the paper are: Nitrocellulose and p-nitrophenyl phosphate.
- These will be taken forward for chemical identity confirmation and EC number lookup in subsequent steps.

This list contains a non-exhaustive inventory of substances that fall within the scope of the European Union's definition of hazardous chemical and biological substances, as provided on: (1) Table 3 of Annex VI to the CLP Regulation 1272/2008/EC; and (2) Annex III of Directive 2000/54/EC (Biological Agents). The EU's Occupational Safety and Health (OSH) Framework Directive 89/391/EEC applies to risks arising from chemical, physical and biological agents at the workplace.

Safety and/or Health Signs at Work Directive
Workplace Signs - minimum requirements & signs on containers and pipes
This list contains hazardous substances based on Table 3 of Annex VI to the CLP Regulation 1272/2008/EC. According to the annexes to Directive 92/58/EEC, storage areas and containers containing chemical substances or mixtures that are classified as hazardous according to the CLP Regulation must be duly marked and/or labeled. Directive 92/58/EEC requires employers to ensure proper signage is posted in areas where hazards cannot be avoided or reduced.

qa_pairs.question: If the agent had searched for enzyme-specific EC databases instead of ECHA at turn 18, what different type of identifier would have likely been retrieved to satisfy the biology-specific context of the task?
qa_pairs.answer: Enzyme Commission numbers for enzymes like alkaline phosphatase (e.g., EC 3.1.3.1) which are used in ELISA protocols.

qa_pairs.question: At turn 15, what specific repository and PDF file did the agent access to begin the detailed extraction of virus testing method details?
At turn 36, the agent performs a state verification. Which specific analysis file was read to ensure the alphabetization of the final result was correct?
Before executing the search for EC numbers at turn 18, what specific metadata regarding usage frequency had to be established from the paper?
qa_pairs.answer: The agent needed to identify the two chemicals explicitly named and used most frequently in the virus testing method sections (Nitrocellulose and p-nitrophenyl phosphate).

qa_pairs.question: What logical precondition regarding chemical nomenclature was required before the agent could successfully retrieve regulatory identifiers from the ECHA database in turns 19 and 20?
qa_pairs.answer: The agent had to resolve the common names found in the paper (like nitrocellulose) into canonical ECHA names (like cellulose nitrate) to match database entries.

qa_pairs.question: After the agent retrieved the EC numbers at turn 20, what internal verification step was mandated by the prompt's constraints before the final answer could be formatted?
qa_pairs.answer: Confirming the alphabetical order of the canonical chemical names to determine the correct sequence of the semicolon-separated numbers.

qa_pairs.question: At which turn did the agent finalize the state of its chemical identity research by saving the 'analysis_step2_canonical_names.md' file?
What is the exact path of the last file saved by the agent to its workspace before issuing the final response at turn 39?
The agent performed multiple search iterations. Counting from turn 0 to turn 39, how many total 'tavily_search' actions were recorded in the state trajectory?
In turn 25, the agent updated its reasoning state regarding the output format. What specific rationale was documented for the alphabetical ordering of the two identified chemicals?
qa_pairs.answer: The agent decided to use canonical ECHA names for ordering (Cellulose nitrate before Disodium 4-nitrophenyl phosphate) to avoid the ambiguity of common synonyms.

qa_pairs.question: Analyze the trajectory to identify the fundamental failure point: why did the agent's output of regulatory EC numbers (618-392-2) differ from the expected ground truth (3.1.3.1)?
qa_pairs.answer: The agent fell into a semantic trap. It interpreted 'EC number' as a chemical regulatory ID from the European Chemicals Agency (ECHA) instead of the Enzyme Commission number used in biochemistry. Despite correctly finding the paper and the ELISA test, it provided IDs for the reagents rather than the enzymes.

qa_pairs.question: What were the key observations recorded between Turn 9 and Turn 16 that shifted the agent's focus away from enzymatic classification and toward chemical identifiers?
qa_pairs.answer: During the PDF extraction, the agent explicitly identified 'nitrocellulose' and 'p-nitrophenyl phosphate' as the primary chemicals. By prioritizing the identification of 'commonly used chemicals' as reagents rather than the 'method's biochemical catalyst,' it locked its research path into the ECHA database early on.
